title: Pressure-Tuned Competing Electronic States in Layered Tellurides
authors: Mahmoud Abdel-Hafiez, Govindaraj Lingannan, D. A. Chareev, A. N. Vasiliev, Anas Abutaha, Kadir Can Dogan, Mehmet Yagmurcukardes, Mehmet Egilmez, Hasan Sahin, Sami El-Khatib
abstract: Layered transition-metal dichalcogenides (TMDs) host competing electronic states that can be tuned by external perturbations, providing a platform to explore the interplay between disorder, electronic structure, and quantum transport. Here we investigate magnetotransport in bulk semiconducting 2H-MoTe2 under hydrostatic pressure. At ambient pressure, transport evolves from high-temperature metallic behavior into activated conduction and ultimately a strongly localized variable-range hopping regime, accompanied by a pronounced magnetotransport anomaly near 45 K and large, nonsaturating magnetoresistance extending up to an unprecedented field of 60 T in semiconducting 2H-MoTe2. Under compression to 15.6 GPa, the insulating state is rapidly suppressed and a low-resistivity regime emerges in which quantum interference dominates, exhibiting a crossover from weak antilocalization (WAL) to weak localization (WL) at low temperatures. A physically motivated phenomenological description captures the magnetoresistance across these regimes and yields a characteristic electronic length scale that remains comparable across the localized and quantum-interference regimes. First-principles calculations reveal a continuous pressure-driven collapse of the bandgap into a semimetallic electronic structure. These results establish a unified picture of pressure-tuned transport spanning hopping and quantum-coherent regimes.
summary: This paper investigates how applying high pressure to 2H-MoTe2 can tune its electronic properties from an insulator to a semimetal. By using extremely high magnetic fields (60 T), the authors track how disorder-driven effects like variable-range hopping transition into quantum-coherent effects like weak localization. The study provides a unified picture of how the electronic bandgap collapses under pressure, driven by a continuous electronic reconstruction.
main_problem: The study aims to understand the pressure-driven evolution of electronic states in bulk 2H-MoTe2, specifically the transition from a localized, semiconducting regime to a semimetallic regime and the interplay between disorder and quantum transport.
main_result: The researchers demonstrate a continuous, pressure-induced collapse of the bandgap and a transition from a strongly localized variable-range hopping regime to a semimetallic regime characterized by a crossover from weak antilocalization to weak localization.
method: The study combines high-pressure magnetotransport measurements (up to 60 T) with first-principles density functional theory (DFT) calculations including spin-orbit coupling.
model_or_system: The physical system is bulk semiconducting 2H-phase Molybdenum ditelluride (MoTe2), a layered transition-metal dichalcogenide. The transport is modeled using frameworks for 3D Mott variable-range hopping, weak localization/antilocalization, and the Nguyen-Spivak-Shklovskii model for quantum interference.
key_observables: Out-of-plane resistivity, magnetoresistance (MR), lattice parameters, and bandgap evolution.
important_parameters: Pressure up to 15.6 GPa, magnetic fields up to 60 T, localization length (~1.99 nm), and a temperature anomaly near 45 K.
main_assumptions: The use of the GGA with PBE functional and DFT-D2 dispersion corrections in calculations; the assumption of a continuous electronic reconstruction rather than an abrupt structural transition.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows XRD, Raman spectra, and ambient pressure transport/MR; Fig 2 displays high-field MR and temperature evolution of MR parameters; Fig 3 presents resistivity under pressure and 3D WAL fits.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of electronic evolution in TMDs, presents ambient pressure transport and magnetotransport data, details the effects of high hydrostatic pressure on resistivity and quantum interference, provides DFT evidence for bandgap collapse, and concludes with a unified transport framework.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
